content.claim: Since the 1990s, school-choice deregulation and within-school class-selection mechanisms in Finland have re-introduced segregation effects despite the formal absence of early academic tracking, with socio-economic achievement gaps increasing in recent assessments and ethnic/spatial segregation growing in urban areas including Helsinki
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Causality between choice deregulation and segregation increases is correlational rather than experimental
Time lag between school-choice policy changes (1990s) and segregation measurement not precisely specified
Mechanisms (residential sorting, language-class stratification, within-school grouping) are documented descriptively but quantitative interaction effects are not fully disentangled
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
